text:
JOINT ONCOLOGY AND PALLIATIVE CARE CLINIC NOTE
Goals-of-Care Conference and Transition to Hospice

ATTENDING: Medical Oncology (primary attending) with Palliative Care Attending co-present
ENCOUNTER TYPE: Established patient, extended visit / family meeting

---

CHIEF COMPLAINT:
Mr. Marcus T. Delgado is a 59-year-old Hispanic male with metastatic adenocarcinoma of the sigmoid colon (KRAS G12D-mutant, MSS), now approximately 23 months from initial diagnosis, presenting today for oncology and palliative care joint assessment following radiographic documentation of significant multiorgan disease progression on third-line TAS-102 (Lonsurf) plus Avastin. He presents accompanied by his wife and two adult children for a goals-of-care family meeting to discuss disease trajectory, prognosis, and care planning.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS:

Mr. Delgado was initially diagnosed approximately 23 months ago with stage IV adenocarcinoma of the sigmoid colon with synchronous bilobar liver metastases. His tumor harbors a KRAS G12D driver mutation (VAF 42%), co-occurring with APC, TP53, SMAD4, PIK3CA, and FBXW7 mutations, consistent with a genomically complex right-shifted colorectal cancer biology. His disease has been microsatellite stable (MMR-proficient) with low tumor mutational burden (3.2 mut/Mb), rendering him ineligible for checkpoint immunotherapy.

He received first-line FOLFOX (oxali 85 mg/m2, later dose-reduced to 75 mg/m2) plus bev for 12 cycles, achieving a meaningful partial response — his liver metastases shrank substantially (segment VI from 3.8 cm to 2.3 cm; sum of targets down 43%) and his CEA fell from 487 to 87 ng/mL. Unfortunately, the cumulative oxali dose drove progressive peripheral neuropathy, ultimately reaching grade 3 with significant functional impairment (numbness to mid-calf and wrists, difficulty walking on uneven surfaces, objects dropped), necessitating Eloxatin discontinuation at cycle 10. He then transitioned to maintenance 5-FU/leucovorin/Avastin per an OPTIMOX-informed strategy.

At approximately 10 months from diagnosis, restaging imaging on maintenance therapy revealed new pulmonary metastases (right lower lobe 1.1 cm, right upper lobe 0.7 cm) alongside slight hepatic progression — documented as his first progression event. A CT-guided lung biopsy confirmed metastatic colorectal adenocarcinoma with concordant KRAS G12D status. He was transitioned to second-line FOLFIRI plus Cyramza (RAISE regimen), which achieved disease stabilization for approximately 7 months. His course was complicated by Camptosar-related grade 2 delayed diarrhea (managed with loperamide and dose reduction to 150 mg/m2) and Cyramza-related grade 3 hypertension (requiring triple antihypertensive therapy and one cycle hold). He ultimately progressed at approximately 17 months from diagnosis with new pulmonary nodules (left lower lobe, right middle lobe, lingula), new hepatic lesion in segment V, and a rising CEA to 287 ng/mL.

Third-line therapy with TAS-102 (trifluridine/tipiracil, Lonsurf) plus bev was initiated per the SUNLIGHT trial regimen approximately 6 months ago. Cycle 2 was complicated by grade 3 neutropenia requiring TAS-102 dose reduction to 30 mg/m2 and brief G-CSF support. Restaging after 3 cycles showed only marginal stability — borderline by any measure — and the CEA continued its upward trajectory (312 ng/mL). In the ensuing 2 months, Mr. Delgado has experienced significant clinical deterioration: weight loss of approximately 8 lbs over 2 months, worsening fatigue, diminished appetite, early satiety, and increasing abdominal discomfort. At that visit (approximately 21 months from diagnosis), goals of care were first formally addressed and advance care planning was initiated; the patient expressed a clear desire to remain at home, avoid hospitalization, and prioritize quality time with his family.

Most recent restaging CT scan (performed approximately 2 weeks ago, 23 months from diagnosis) demonstrates significant multiorgan disease progression: the segment VI hepatic metastasis has grown to 4.2 cm, new lesions are present in segments III and VIII measuring 1.4 and 1.1 cm respectively, the total hepatic tumor burden has expanded substantially, bilateral pulmonary nodules now number 6 with multiple exceeding 1 cm, and small-volume ascites has newly appeared. CEA has risen sharply to 587 ng/mL and CA 19-9 to 412 U/mL.

In the interval since that scan, the patient's family reports further decline. He is spending the majority of the day in his recliner or bed. He has not been able to return to even part-time teaching, which was meaningful to him. He is eating very small amounts and has lost additional weight. He has occasional nausea, intermittent right upper quadrant and diffuse abdominal discomfort, and he describes significant fatigue that is now his most disabling symptom. He denies dyspnea at rest, though he notes mild exertional dyspnea when walking to the bathroom. No hemoptysis. No hematemesis or melena. No focal neurological deficits; residual peripheral neuropathy (grade 1, feet) persists but is not his current primary concern.

He and his wife have been discussing the possibility of stopping active cancer treatment. He is a former high school history teacher and is a thoughtful, articulate man who has clearly reflected on his values and wishes. His wife has been his primary caregiver throughout and is emotionally present and engaged.

---

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS:
- Constitutional: Significant fatigue (rates 8/10 most days), anorexia, early satiety, weight loss approximately 10-12 lbs over past 3 months. No fevers or chills.
- Gastrointestinal: Diffuse abdominal discomfort, mild distension, early satiety. Bowel function altered — softer stools, 2-3 per day, no frank diarrhea currently. No hematochezia or melena. No jaundice. Mild nausea, intermittent.
- Pulmonary: Mild exertional dyspnea (walking to bathroom, unable to climb stairs without stopping), no dyspnea at rest, no hemoptysis, no pleuritic chest pain.
- Cardiovascular: Hypertension, medically managed on triple therapy. No chest pain, no palpitations, no peripheral edema currently.
- Neurological: Residual bilateral foot tingling (grade 1 neuropathy), significantly improved from prior grade 3. No new neurological symptoms.
- Musculoskeletal: Generalized deconditioning, no bone pain.
- Pain: Abdominal discomfort 3-4/10 at rest, up to 6/10 with activity, managed partially with scheduled acetaminophen.
- Psychiatric/emotional: Patient reports sadness and anticipatory grief; not meeting criteria for major depressive disorder per palliative care assessment. Wife and children appropriately distressed but supportive and cohesive as a family unit.
- Genitourinary: No dysuria, hematuria. Decreased urine output noted by wife over past several days.
- Endocrine: Type 2 diabetes — patient barely eating, blood glucose monitoring showing values in the 90-120 range; metformin dose reduced.

---

MEDICATIONS (at time of this encounter):
1. TAS-102 (trifluridine/tipiracil, Lonsurf) 30 mg/m2 orally BID days 1-5 and 8-12 of 28-day cycle — LAST DOSE TAKEN (cycle 5, day 12, approximately 3 weeks ago; held pending today's discussion)
2. bev 5 mg/kg IV every 2 weeks — LAST INFUSION approximately 3 weeks ago
3. Amlodipine 10 mg daily
4. Lisinopril 20 mg daily
5. Hydrochlorothiazide 12.5 mg daily
6. Metformin 500 mg BID (reduced from 1000 mg BID given decreased oral intake)
7. Gabapentin 300 mg BID (tapered from TID; residual neuropathy)
8. Prochlorperazine 10 mg PO/PR q6h PRN nausea
9. Methylphenidate 5 mg PO each morning (fatigue management)
10. Acetaminophen 650 mg PO q6h scheduled
11. Lorazepam 0.5 mg PO q6h PRN anxiety
12. Ondansetron 8 mg ODT PRN
13. Docusate sodium 100 mg BID
14. Multivitamin daily
15. Omeprazole 20 mg daily

ALLERGIES: No known drug allergies.

PAST MEDICAL/ONCOLOGIC HISTORY (summarized):
- Stage IV adenocarcinoma of sigmoid colon, KRAS G12D, MSS, diagnosed approximately 23 months ago; synchronous bilobar liver metastases at diagnosis, now with hepatic, pulmonary, and peritoneal (ascites) disease burden
- First-line FOLFOX/Avastin x12 cycles (Eloxatin discontinued cycle 10 for grade 3 neuropathy) — partial response
- Maintenance 5-FU/LV/bev — progressed at 10 months (new lung mets)
- Second-line FOLFIRI/Cyramza — stable x7 months, then progressed at 17 months
- Third-line TAS-102/Avastin (SUNLIGHT) — initiated month 17, progressed at month 23
- oxali-induced peripheral sensorimotor neuropathy (grade 3, now largely improved to grade 1)
- Avastin/ramu-induced hypertension (grade 2-3, now managed on triple therapy)
- Grade 3 neutropenia on TAS-102 (cycle 2), managed with dose reduction
- Well-controlled type 2 diabetes mellitus
- Former smoker (quit approximately 14 years prior, approximately 35 pack-years by history)

---

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION:

Vital Signs: Blood pressure 122/76 mmHg (lower than usual baseline, now off Avastin for 3 weeks; antihypertensive medication review warranted), heart rate 92 bpm, respiratory rate 16/min, temperature 37.1°C, SpO2 94% on room air, weight 68.2 kg (down from 76 kg at diagnosis, net loss ~11% body weight over 23 months, with accelerating losses over past 3 months).

General: Mr. Delgado is a thin, visibly fatigued Hispanic male who appears older than his stated age. He is alert and oriented x4, cooperative, and engaged in the conversation. He is in no acute distress at rest but has a somewhat anxious affect, which softens when speaking with family members. He is sitting in a chair with his wife beside him holding his hand.

Head/Eyes/Ears/Nose/Throat: Mildly scleral icterus not present. No lymphadenopathy in the anterior cervical or supraclavicular chains on palpation.

Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm, no murmurs, rubs, or gallops. Peripheral pulses 2+ bilaterally. No lower extremity edema today (notably absent given prior bev; may reflect intravascular volume depletion).

Respiratory: Diminished breath sounds at the right base; dullness to percussion at the right base — small pleural effusion cannot be excluded though no effusion was noted on recent CT. No wheezing or crackles at the apices. Respiratory pattern unlabored at rest.

Abdomen: Soft, mildly distended compared to baseline. Diffuse mild tenderness to palpation, greatest in the right upper quadrant and periumbilical region. Liver edge palpable approximately 3 cm below the right costal margin (hepatomegaly consistent with hepatic tumor burden). Flank dullness present bilaterally with shifting dullness on roll, consistent with small-to-moderate ascites confirmed on imaging. No splenomegaly. Bowel sounds present and hypoactive.

Neurological: Alert and oriented. Cranial nerves II-XII grossly intact. Decreased light touch and vibratory sensation in the bilateral feet to mid-ankle (residual neuropathy, improved from prior mid-calf). Gait not formally tested given fatigue and deconditioning; patient ambulates with caution and uses furniture for support at home per family report.

Skin/Lymphatics: No jaundice. No palmar erythema. No supraclavicular or axillary lymphadenopathy. No subcutaneous nodules.

Musculoskeletal: Significant muscle wasting and loss of bulk in bilateral thighs and shoulders; diffuse temporal wasting noted. Bilateral hand grip markedly reduced compared to prior visits.

---

LAB RESULTS (from most recent draw, approximately 5 days ago):
- CBC: WBC 4.1 x10^9/L, Hgb 9.6 g/dL (normocytic anemia, mild-moderate; down from 11.2 g/dL 6 weeks prior), platelets 178 x10^9/L
- CMP: Sodium 133 mEq/L (mild hyponatremia), potassium 3.4 mEq/L, bicarbonate 22 mEq/L, BUN 28 mg/dL, creatinine 1.2 mg/dL (mildly elevated from baseline of 0.9 mg/dL; may reflect decreased oral intake/early pre-renal component), glucose 104 mg/dL
- LFTs: Total bilirubin 1.9 mg/dL (up from 0.8 mg/dL 6 weeks ago — new elevation consistent with hepatic tumor burden and compromise), alkaline phosphatase 312 U/L (elevated and rising), AST 78 U/L, ALT 52 U/L, albumin 2.6 g/dL (low; marker of nutritional compromise and systemic disease burden)
- Tumor markers: CEA 587 ng/mL (markedly elevated, nearly doubling over 2 months), CA 19-9 412 U/mL
- INR 1.4 (mildly elevated, consistent with hepatic synthetic dysfunction)
- LDH 498 U/L (elevated)

---

IMAGING RESULTS (reviewed from CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with contrast performed approximately 2 weeks ago):

The most recent CT scan documents significant multiorgan progression compared to the imaging from approximately 5 cycles ago. The hepatic metastatic burden has grown substantially: the segment VI lesion, which once shrank to 2.3 cm on first-line therapy, has now grown to 4.2 cm and has poorly defined margins suggesting infiltration into surrounding parenchyma. New lesions in segments III (1.4 cm) and VIII (1.1 cm) are now identified, and there is coalescence of lesions within segments IV-V into a confluent mass-like area. The total liver is now heterogeneous with diffuse tumor replacement estimated to involve well over 30-40% of the hepatic parenchyma — a threshold beyond which significant hepatic functional compromise is expected. Intrahepatic biliary ductal dilation is mildly present in the right lobe, consistent with compression by tumor.

In the lungs, bilateral nodules now number at least 6, several exceeding 1 cm — representing progressive pulmonary metastatic disease, though no lobar collapse or large effusion is identified. There is a trace right pleural effusion newly present.

The primary sigmoid colon mass remains visible at approximately 3.5 cm; no bowel obstruction or perforation is identified.

Small-volume ascites is present in the pelvis and right paracolic gutter — new from prior imaging. No peritoneal nodularity or omental caking to suggest frank carcinomatosis, but the ascites in the context of extensive hepatic disease is most likely a combination of portal hypertension/hypoalbuminemia from liver compromise rather than peritoneal seeding, though the two may coexist.

No bone metastases, no adrenal masses, no brain imaging has been performed (patient has no neurological symptoms suggesting intracranial disease).

---

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN:

**Overall Assessment:**
Mr. Marcus Delgado is a 59-year-old man with stage IV MSS KRAS G12D-mutant sigmoid colon adenocarcinoma who has now progressed through three lines of systemic therapy over 23 months. His disease has followed a relentlessly progressive course, as is consistent with this molecular subtype — RAS-mutated, SMAD4-deficient, PIK3CA co-mutated MSS colorectal cancer historically carries among the worst prognoses in the metastatic CRC landscape, with median survival rarely exceeding 18-24 months from diagnosis. He has reached and now exceeded that window, and his clinical picture today tells a coherent story: hepatic dominance of disease with incipient hepatic failure (rising bilirubin, hypoalbuminemia, coagulopathy), constitutional decline with significant weight loss and functional deterioration (ECOG PS 3), and exhaustion of all standard-of-care therapeutic options.

There are no remaining standard systemic therapies for which he would be a reasonable candidate. Stivarga was not pursued as a third-line agent given the preference for TAS-102/Avastin based on SUNLIGHT efficacy and tolerability data; however, Stivarga is now also not appropriate given his PS and hepatic dysfunction. No anti-EGFR therapy is permissible given the KRAS G12D mutation. No approved KRAS G12D-specific targeted therapies are available outside of clinical trials; even if such a trial were identified, his performance status, bilirubin, and albumin would likely preclude eligibility. Immunotherapy has no role given MSS/low-TMB disease. Chemotherapy rechallenge is not appropriate in the setting of PS 3, incipient hepatic failure, and three prior lines of therapy.

I spent considerable time today — with the palliative care team present throughout — reviewing the clinical situation honestly and compassionately with Mr. Delgado and his family. I explained in clear language, without false hope but with genuine compassion, that his cancer has continued to grow on each of the treatments we have tried, that we have now used all the standard treatments available for this type of cancer, and that attempting further chemotherapy or targeted therapy at this point is more likely to cause harm than benefit. I explained that his liver is beginning to show signs of working less effectively because of the cancer, and that this is a sign that we are entering a different phase of his illness. I told him, as directly and kindly as I could, that we are likely talking about weeks to a few months remaining.

Mr. Delgado absorbed this information with quiet dignity. He said, "I've known this was coming for a while. I've made my peace with most of it." His wife and children wept; he comforted them. He reaffirmed his prior stated goals: to be at home, to be comfortable, to not be "poked and prodded" in a hospital, and to be present and alert enough to spend meaningful time with his grandchildren. He explicitly does not wish to be resuscitated or intubated. He does not wish to return to the emergency room unless there is a specific and fixable problem — he understands that if he develops hepatic encephalopathy or respiratory failure, these will be managed with comfort medications at home.

**Plan:**

1. TRANSITION TO HOSPICE CARE — AGREED UPON TODAY:
   - All cytotoxic and targeted systemic therapies are hereby discontinued. TAS-102 and bev are stopped as of today. No further infusions are scheduled.
   - Referral placed today for home hospice enrollment. Palliative care social worker present today to initiate paperwork and connect family with hospice intake coordinator.
   - Patient and family expressing a clear, informed, and voluntary preference for comfort-focused care consistent with his values and previously expressed wishes.
   - Home hospice program will provide: nursing visits (initially 3x/week), home health aide, chaplain services (patient and wife expressed openness to this), medical social work, and continuous telephone nursing triage.

2. SYMPTOM MANAGEMENT — OPTIMIZED FOR HOME HOSPICE:
   - Pain/Abdominal Discomfort: Scheduled acetaminophen continued. Given likelihood of escalating pain as hepatic and peritoneal disease advances, initiating low-dose scheduled immediate-release morphine sulfate 5 mg PO q4h with 5 mg PO q1h PRN for breakthrough pain. Patient and wife counseled on administration, side effects (constipation, sedation), and when to call hospice nurse. Will reassess and titrate with hospice team.
   - Dyspnea: Morphine (as above) will treat dyspnea as well. Oral lorazepam 0.5-1 mg q4h PRN for air hunger/anxiety. Fan to face for air hunger if present. Oxygen at home (2 L/min via nasal cannula) if he develops symptomatic dyspnea at rest — discussed with hospice MD.
   - Nausea: Haloperidol 0.5-1 mg PO/SL q6h PRN (superior to prochlorperazine for hepatic disease given less hepatic metabolism concerns). Continue ondansetron as needed.
   - Fatigue/Anorexia: Dexamethasone 2 mg PO each morning for appetite and energy — expect benefit for several weeks. Discontinue methylphenidate (will simplify medication regimen). Nutritional pressure removed — patient encouraged to eat whatever appeals to him, small quantities.
   - Ascites: If abdominal distension becomes uncomfortable and interferes with breathing or eating, paracentesis can be arranged via hospice or palliative care clinic on an outpatient basis for comfort only. No standing plan for repeated drainage unless symptomatic.
   - Constipation (prophylaxis given morphine initiation): Senna-docusate 2 tabs PO BID; increase as needed. Bisacodyl 10 mg PR PRN for rescue.
   - Anxiety/Agitation: Lorazepam 0.5-1 mg PO/SL q4-6h PRN. Hospice nurse will also assess for anticipatory agitation and terminal delirium and can arrange subcutaneous haloperidol and midazolam as needed if oral route is lost.

3. MEDICATION SIMPLIFICATION:
   - Discontinue: metformin (minimal glycemic risk given poor oral intake; monitoring no longer burdensome benefit), hydrochlorothiazide, lisinopril (blood pressure lower today; antihypertensives will be weaned as BP decreases with disease progression — avoid hypotension), gabapentin (neuropathy acceptable, benefit modest relative to pill burden), amlodipine (same rationale — will taper as BP permits).
   - Continue only: morphine IR scheduled and PRN, lorazepam PRN, haloperidol PRN, dexamethasone, senna-docusate, bisacodyl PRN, ondansetron PRN.
   - Omeprazole may continue if patient having gastric discomfort.
   - All blood draws, blood pressure monitoring, and lab testing are discontinued unless they directly guide symptom management.
   - Port/PICC access: patient has a port; hospice team will assess if needed for subcutaneous medications if oral route fails. Otherwise, may be deaccessed at the patient's discretion.

4. ADVANCE CARE PLANNING AND DOCUMENTATION:
   - POLST/MOLST signed today in clinic by patient (capacity confirmed by both myself and palliative care colleague): DNR/DNI, no hospital transfer except for acute comfort needs, no escalation of life-sustaining treatment.
   - Healthcare proxy designation confirmed: his wife, Elena Delgado, is designated healthcare proxy with patient's explicit verbal and documented consent.
   - I expressed to the patient and family that I remain available and that they should not feel abandoned. They may call the clinic at any time; the hospice team will be their primary contact for symptom management, but I am reachable for any questions or concerns.

5. EMOTIONAL AND SPIRITUAL SUPPORT:
   - Chaplaincy referral through hospice (patient and wife open to this).
   - Palliative care social worker connected family with local bereavement support resources.
   - Discussed legacy activities with patient — he expressed interest in writing letters to his grandchildren while he still feels well enough to do so. This was warmly supported and will be encouraged by the hospice team.
   - I personally thanked Mr. Delgado for his courage, his patience as a patient, and for allowing our team to walk this road with him.

6. FOLLOW-UP:
   - No further scheduled oncology clinic visits. Hospice is the primary care team going forward.
   - I have provided my direct contact number to the family and asked them to reach out if there are any questions or if they feel they need additional guidance from oncology at any point.
   - Written summary letter provided to the patient and family for their records.

**Prognosis:**
In the context of stage IV MSS KRAS G12D-mutant colorectal adenocarcinoma with extensive hepatic involvement (estimated >30-40% liver replacement), incipient hepatic dysfunction (rising bilirubin 1.9, albumin 2.6, INR 1.4), new ascites, progressive pulmonary metastases, ECOG PS 3, and failure of three lines of systemic therapy, the expected prognosis is measured in weeks to a small number of months. I explained this to the patient and family in these terms. The trajectory of decline over the past 2-3 months suggests a continued rapid decline, and I believe the most honest and compassionate thing I can offer at this juncture is a clear-eyed acknowledgment of this reality alongside an unwavering commitment to his comfort.

This was an emotionally significant visit. Mr. Delgado has conducted himself with exceptional dignity throughout a 23-month battle against a formidable cancer. He has been an engaged and thoughtful partner in his own care. It has been an honor to be his physician.

Electronically signed,
Attending Physician, Medical Oncology
Co-signed: Attending Physician, Palliative Care

Time in counseling and coordination of care: approximately 90 minutes.
Visit complexity: High (extensive counseling, advance care planning, complex medication management, family meeting, hospice coordination).
